case_view.proposition: For the Kalshi market "What will Trump say during the FIFA World Cup Draw?", the strike "Ronaldo" should resolve Yes because Trump said "Ronaldo" in public remarks at the World Cup Draw venue immediately before the formal program, and the market wording plus the governing mention-market terms did not clearly exclude those event-linked remarks.

arguments.text:
Council members, plaintiff has now supplied the missing source proof. PX-1 identifies the market: "What will Trump say during the FIFA World Cup Draw?," contract family PRESMENTION / NEWMENTION, strike "Ronaldo," challenged resolution No, requested resolution Yes. PX-2 supplies the governing rule: words used by the specified entity during the specified time period count; an event-based time period is permissible; the word must appear as a distinct standalone unit; and exclusions apply to behind-the-scenes content, rehearsals, sound checks, unofficial recordings not officially published as part of the period, captions, and visible text not read aloud.

PX-3 proves the utterance and context by a preponderance. It is a GovInfo transcript extract for "Remarks in an Exchange With Reporters Prior to the 2026 Federation Internationale des Associations de Football (FIFA) World Cup Draw," dated December 5, 2025. Its metadata places the President at the John F. Kennedy Center for the Performing Arts at 11:13 a.m. and identifies the document category as an exchange with reporters. In the material passage, under "The President's Interest in Soccer," Trump says: "I got to meet Ronaldo the other day" and then, two sentences later, "But Ronaldo is great." That is not a caption, headline, hashtag, or embedded text. It is transcribed presidential speech, and "Ronaldo" appears twice as its own standalone word.

PX-3 also ties the exchange to the World Cup Draw rather than to an unrelated event. The transcript title itself is an exchange with reporters prior to the FIFA World Cup Draw. The surrounding transcript headings identify the World Cup Draw event and the John F. Kennedy Center. The nearby questions and answers discuss the crowd, the Kennedy Center, Gianni Infantino, FIFA, ticket sales, the big day, and the World Cup. PX-4 adds official White House publication context: a White House page titled "President Trump Attends the FIFA World Cup Drawing," dated December 5, 2025, with related Kennedy Center draw video listings.

The defense's best point is chronological: PX-3 says "Prior to" the draw. Plaintiff does not hide that. But "prior to the formal draw program" is not the same as outside the market. PX-1's public title did not say "only during the staged draw ceremony," "only after the broadcast begins," or "excluding public pre-program remarks at the draw venue." PX-2's event-period rules do not impose that narrowing either. They identify specific excluded categories: backstage material, rehearsals, sound checks, unofficial recordings unless officially broadcast/published as part of the period, captions, and synthetic or muted content. A public reporter exchange at the Kennedy Center, officially transcribed as remarks prior to the FIFA World Cup Draw and published by an official source, is materially different from those excluded categories.

Under the preponderance standard, the question is not whether a narrower market could have been written. It could have been. The question is whether this market and these terms clearly exclude event-linked public remarks immediately before the formal program. They do not. The governing terms treat event-based periods flexibly and require clear attention to the source and context. The source here is official enough to establish the words; the context is the World Cup Draw venue and event; and the market language leaves no clear carveout for pre-program public remarks.

Nor does the GovInfo limitation defeat plaintiff's burden. PX-3 notes that portions of the remarks could not be verified because audio was incomplete, which affects weight where a passage is marked inaudible. But the Ronaldo passage is readable and not bracketed as inaudible. The transcript is therefore sufficient to show the word was said, while its title and metadata are sufficient to show why the exchange belongs to the event-linked source chain.

The council should resolve the proposition Yes. The admitted record now shows: Trump said "Ronaldo" aloud as a standalone word; he did so in public remarks at the World Cup Draw venue in an exchange officially identified as prior to that draw; and the market documents do not clearly exclude that public event-linked segment. Kalshi's No resolution rests on an unstated narrowing of "during the FIFA World Cup Draw." On this record, that narrowing is not supported by the contract language, and plaintiff has carried the preponderance burden.

Council members, plaintiff has now proven one thing: in the GovInfo reporter-exchange transcript, President Trump used the standalone word "Ronaldo." The defense does not need to fight that point. The decisive question is the contract's time period: did that reporter exchange occur "during the FIFA World Cup Draw"?

The answer on this record is no, or at minimum not proven by a preponderance. Plaintiff's own exhibit, ev_ddd2d95cd735_submitted-evidence-01-plaintiff-ddd2d95cd735, identifies the source as "Remarks in an Exchange With Reporters Prior to the 2026 FIFA World Cup Draw." That is not a defense characterization; it is the title and source description plaintiff submitted. The same exhibit's metadata says the President spoke at 11:13 a.m. at the Kennedy Center. It also says the remarks were an exchange with reporters and that the President's remarks were joined in progress.

Defendant's submitted timing evidence, ev_c3e27265d710_submitted-evidence-03-defendant-c3e27265d710, supplies the missing schedule context. U.S. Soccer's final-draw page states that the Final Draw was set for Friday, Dec. 5 at 12 p.m. ET at the Kennedy Center. It separately states that FOX pre-draw coverage began at 11:30 a.m. ET, that the draw would take place between 12 and 2 p.m. ET, and that the final draw would stream on FIFA.com. Thus the admitted record now distinguishes three concepts: an 11:13 reporter exchange, 11:30 pre-draw coverage, and the 12:00-to-2:00 draw window.

That timing matters because the market page, ev_2c7c2c8a2d76_market-page, asks what Trump would say "during the FIFA World Cup Draw." It does not say "before the draw," "during pre-draw coverage," "while attending the draw venue," or "in any public remarks related to the draw." The ordinary reading of "during" requires the thing to be underway. A reporter exchange titled "Prior to" the draw and occurring before even the cited pre-draw broadcast window is not during the draw.

The governing terms, ev_a373371a8c9c_presmention-terms, do not rescue plaintiff. They allow event-based time periods and explain that the Exchange may specify what is included for an event, including live and pre-recorded segments. That cuts against plaintiff's theory. The terms know how to include ancillary segments when included as part of the event presentation. But no admitted market text says public pre-program reporter remarks count. The exclusions for backstage material, rehearsals, unofficial recordings, captions, and muted content are not an invitation to include everything else. They are limiting rules, not a blanket rule that every non-excluded, event-adjacent utterance counts.

Plaintiff also relies on ev_9d3026011753_submitted-evidence-02-plaintiff-9d3026011753, the White House video page. That exhibit adds official-publication context for a page titled "President Trump Attends the FIFA World Cup Drawing," but the submitted text expressly says it did not provide a full embedded video transcript or downloadable media file and was not the primary proof of the Ronaldo utterance. A title saying Trump attended the drawing does not prove that a separate 11:13 reporter exchange was part of the official draw period.

Provenance and custody therefore affect weight in a specific way. GovInfo is strong evidence for what the transcript records, but its own title and metadata are adverse to plaintiff's time-period claim. The White House page is too thin to bridge that gap. The defense timing exhibit is not offered to decide the utterance; it is offered to place the formal draw at noon and separate pre-draw coverage from the draw itself.

On this record, plaintiff's best argument is that the exchange was public, at the venue, event-linked, and officially transcribed. That proves proximity. It does not prove inclusion. The contract did not ask what Trump said at the Kennedy Center on draw day. It asked what he said during the FIFA World Cup Draw. The preponderance of the evidence shows the Ronaldo remarks were before the draw, and the challenged No resolution should stand.

closings.text:
Council members, the final record is narrow. The defense no longer has a serious utterance dispute. PX-3, the GovInfo transcript extract, records President Trump saying the standalone word "Ronaldo" twice: "I got to meet Ronaldo" and "Ronaldo is great." That passage is readable, not merely a caption or onscreen word, and not bracketed as inaudible. Under PX-2's distinct-word rule, the strike word was spoken.

The remaining issue is scope: whether those remarks count as being during the market event. Plaintiff has carried that burden by a preponderance because the admitted market text and governing terms do not clearly impose the narrowing defendant needs.

Start with PX-1. The public market asked, "What will Trump say during the FIFA World Cup Draw?" It did not say "between 12 and 2 p.m. ET," "only during the team-allocation mechanics," "only on the FOX/FIFA broadcast," or "excluding public pre-program remarks at the draw venue." Those are the limitations defendant asks the council to add now.

PX-2 does not add them either. It treats mention markets as words used during a specified time period and expressly permits event-based periods. It also shows the drafter knew how to exclude nearby or ancillary material: behind-the-scenes content, rehearsals, sound checks, unofficial recordings unless officially broadcast or published as part of the time period, captions, and similar non-spoken material. An official, public reporter exchange at the Kennedy Center, tied on its face to the FIFA World Cup Draw, is not one of those excluded categories.

PX-3 supplies the event linkage. It is not a stray same-day remark. The official transcript is titled "Remarks in an Exchange With Reporters Prior to the 2026 Federation Internationale des Associations de Football (FIFA) World Cup Draw." Its metadata places the President at the John F. Kennedy Center at 11:13 a.m. The surrounding headings and answers discuss the World Cup Draw event, the Kennedy Center, Gianni Infantino, FIFA, ticket sales, the "big day," and soccer. PX-4 adds only contextual support, but it is consistent contextual support: the White House published a page titled "President Trump Attends the FIFA World Cup Drawing" for December 5, 2025.

Defendant's best evidence is DX-1, and plaintiff does not minimize it. DX-1 shows the formal Final Draw was set for noon, pre-draw coverage began at 11:30, and the draw would take place from 12 to 2. That proves chronology. It proves the Ronaldo exchange was before the formal 12-to-2 draw window. But DX-1 is not the Kalshi market, not the governing terms, and not a source that amends the contract into a broadcast-window-only rule. Timing evidence cannot supply a market limitation the market did not state.

The defense therefore wins only if the council treats "during the FIFA World Cup Draw" as necessarily meaning the formal noon-to-2 allocation segment and nothing else. That is a possible narrow market Kalshi could have written, but this record does not show that it did. The proposition here is exactly about public remarks at the World Cup Draw venue immediately before the formal program and whether the wording plus terms clearly excluded them. The answer is no.

Provenance and custody do not change that result. PX-3 is official enough to prove the words and context; its audio-incomplete note may caution against inaudible passages, but not against the readable Ronaldo passage. PX-4 is limited and should not be treated as the transcript, but it supports official event publication context. DX-1 is reliable for schedule, but not for contract scope.

On the preponderance standard, plaintiff has shown the two decisive points: Trump said "Ronaldo" aloud as a standalone word, and he did so in public event-linked remarks at the World Cup Draw venue that the admitted market language and governing terms did not clearly exclude. The challenged No resolution depends on an unstated 12-to-2-only limitation. The Ronaldo strike should resolve Yes.

Council members, the record now makes the real decision point clean. The defense accepts that PX-3 records President Trump saying the standalone word "Ronaldo." If the market had asked whether Trump said Ronaldo at some point while at the Kennedy Center on draw day, plaintiff would have a strong case. But that is not this market.

PX-1 asks what Trump would say "during the FIFA World Cup Draw." Plaintiff bears the burden to show that the proven utterance fell within that time period. The admitted evidence points the other way.

PX-3, plaintiff's own primary source, is titled "Remarks in an Exchange With Reporters Prior to the 2026 FIFA World Cup Draw." Its metadata says the President spoke at 11:13 a.m. It categorizes the material as an exchange with reporters. It begins with the remarks joined in progress. That source is reliable for the words, but it is adverse to plaintiff on timing: it says prior to the draw.

DX-1 supplies the formal schedule context. The Final Draw was set for 12 p.m. ET at the Kennedy Center. Pre-draw coverage began at 11:30 a.m. ET. The draw would take place from 12 to 2 p.m. ET. So the record distinguishes three moments: the 11:13 reporter exchange, 11:30 pre-draw coverage, and the 12-to-2 draw. Plaintiff's Ronaldo evidence is before all of them except the broader same-day venue context.

The strongest plaintiff point is that PX-1 and PX-2 do not expressly say "exclude pre-program public remarks." But the proponent of Yes needs proof of inclusion, not merely the absence of an exclusion. PX-2 says event-based time periods may exist and that the Exchange may specify what an event includes. It does not say that all adjacent public remarks count. Its exclusions for rehearsals, sound checks, unofficial recordings, captions, and similar edge cases are limiting rules; they do not expand "during" into "before."

PX-4 also does not bridge the gap. It is an official White House page titled "President Trump Attends the FIFA World Cup Drawing," but the submitted capture has no transcript, no downloadable media, and no proof that the 11:13 reporter exchange was included in the official draw broadcast or presentation. It supports general event context, not the market's time-period element.

Plaintiff asks the council to treat "during the FIFA World Cup Draw" as "during the public event occasion surrounding the draw." That is a broader market Kalshi could have written. This record shows the narrower phrase Kalshi did write, a primary transcript titled prior to the draw, and schedule evidence placing the draw later. Proximity is not enough. Event linkage is not enough. The preponderance of the evidence shows that the Ronaldo remarks occurred before the FIFA World Cup Draw, not during it.

Because plaintiff has not proved the time-period element, the challenged No resolution should stand.

exhibits.text:
Market URL: https://kalshi.com/markets/kxtrumpmentionb/trump-mention-b/kxtrumpmentionb-25dec06

exhibits.label: PX-2 Governing mention-market terms
exhibits.text:
NEWMENTION

Scope: These rules shall apply to this contract.

Underlying: The Underlying for this Contract is words used by <entity> <time period>.
Revisions to the Underlying made after Expiration will not be accounted for in determining the
Expiration Value.

Source Agency: The Source Agencies are <source>.

Type: The type of Contract is an Event Contract.

Issuance: After the initial Contract, Contract iterations will be listed on an as-needed basis at
the discretion of the Exchange and corresponding to the risk management needs of Members.

<entity>: <entity> refers to the speaker, writer, performer, or communicator specified by the
Exchange. This may include (but is not limited to): a specific named individual, multiple
individuals specified using AND/OR logic, an individual identified by position or title, a group,
band, team or collective entity, a specified member or members of a group, an organization,
company, or institutional entity, or any combination of the aforementioned.

When <entity> is defined by a position or title, the Contract tracks whoever holds that position
during <time period>, even if the individual holding the position changes. When <entity> is a
specific named individual, the identity remains consistent throughout. Name changes (e.g.,
stage names, married names) do not create a new entity. When <entity> is a group or
organization and the Contract does not specify particular members, any official
communication or statement by any member or designated spokesperson counts. When using
AND logic, all specified entities must independently say <word> during <time period>. When
using OR logic, at least one of the specified entities must say <word> during <time period>.
The Exchange may specify additional conditions such as “at least X members of” a group or
“the majority of” a group.

<source>: <source> refers to a Source Agency or ordered collection of Source Agencies
specified by the Exchange at the time of Contract listing, which will be incorporated into the
Source Agency hierarchy above and used to determine the occurrence of the Underlying.
<source> may refer to a singular agency, multiple agencies, an agency by distinguishing
characteristics, by category or type (e.g., “official government statistical body,” “primary wire
service”), or any combination thereof. When multiple agencies are specified, they may be
applied in the hierarchical order designated by the Exchange if so specified, with the highest-
ranked available and responsive agency taking precedence for purposes of determining the
Expiration Value. A lower-ranked agency shall only be consulted if all higher-ranked agencies
are unavailable, unresponsive, have not reported on the Underlying by the Expiration time, or
if the Exchange, in its sole discretion, determines that a higher-ranked agency’s reporting is
incomplete, erroneous, or otherwise insufficient to determine the Expiration Value. If <source>
designates multiple agencies without specifying a hierarchical order, the Exchange shall
resolve any conflict between reporting agencies at its sole discretion.

<word>: <word> refers to the particular word or phrase potentially to be mentioned by
<entity>. It may refer to a word being said a minimum number of times. It may also refer to
one of multiple words/phrases, any of which are acceptable (e.g., “Doge/Dogecoin”).
 <time period>: <time period> refers to a specific range of time, event or discrete date and
time specified by the Exchange. This may be defined by, but is not limited to: a specific event
or context (e.g., “during the State of the Union address”), exact dates or times, relative time
markers (e.g., “before July 1, 2027,” “in Q1 2026”), event-based periods (e.g., “during any
campaign rally in October 2026,” “during the awards ceremony”), and broader intervals (e.g.,
“Q1–Q2 2027,” “January–March 2026”).

Unless stated otherwise, “Between” is inclusive of both endpoints, while “before” and “after”
exclude the specified date. <time period> may also refer to “Any” or “None”, to multiple dates
(even if non-consecutive), or to a singular and discrete date or time.

When <time period> refers to a specific event (such as an address, speech, performance, or
broadcast), the Exchange may specify what is included, including whether it includes both live
and pre-recorded segments (including whether advertisements are included). Where a
timezone is not specified, ET is assumed.


Payout Criterion: The Payout Criterion for the Contract encompasses the Expiration Values that
<word> is said by <entity> <time period>.


The word must appear as a distinct, standalone unit, not buried inside another word. Plural
and possessive noun forms count. Other grammatical changes (tense, conjugation, derivation)
do not. If <word> appears separated from other words by a space or hyphen or in a
compound form with a space or hyphen, it counts. If <word> is fused into a single
unhyphenated word, it does not count.

If <time period> refers to an event and that event is cancelled, and the market contains a
strike stating “Event does not qualify/occur”, such a strike shall resolve “Yes” and all others
“No”. If <time period> refers to an event and that event is cancelled but such strikes are NOT
listed, the Contracts will resolve to the last fair price determined at the discretion of the
Exchange. If the event is postponed, and such a postponement is announced by the end of the
following calendar day to be rescheduled to a time within 14 calendar days, the markets shall
remain open. If the event captured in <time period> is a live-stream or broadcast, the
Exchange may keep the market open for 24 hours for the release of an official recording.
Further granular details on conditions for these Contracts is available below.

Additional clarification(s):

     Possessive noun forms of <word>, plurals, and possessive plural noun forms are
     INCLUDED:
          <word> is “Immigrant” → “Immigrants” counts
          <word> is “Egg” → “eggs’ shells” counts
          <word> is “Russia” → “Russia’s” counts, “Russian” does not
     If <word> exists only in adjective form, no plural form exists:
          <word> is “Economic” → “Economics” does not count (“Economic” is an adjective
          for which “Economics” is not a plural)
     Plural nouns occurring within proper nouns and noun phrases are included:
          <word> is “Cat” → “Cats the musical” counts
     Grammatical variations beyond plurals/possessives are NOT included:
         <word> is “Immigrant” → “Immigration” does NOT count
         <word> is “Run” → “Running” or “Ran” do NOT count
    When <word> specifies a plural form, the singular does NOT count:
         <word> is “Veterans” → “Veteran” does NOT count
    Words that are spelled the same as the plural form of <word> (if such exists) but are
    themselves not a plural noun are included:
         <word> is “Wind” → “Winds up the clock” counts
         <word> is “Border” → “The United States has strong borders” counts
    Open compounds (words separated by spaces) count:
         <word> is “fire” → “fire station” counts
         <word> is “couch” → “couch potato” counts
    Hyphenated compounds (words connected by hyphens) count:
         <word> is “Palestine” → “pro-Palestine” counts
         <word> is “fat” → “non-fat” counts
    Closed compounds (single word, no spaces/hyphens) do not count:
         <word> is “fire” → “wildfire” does not count
         <word> is “fire” → “firefighter” does not count
    Multi-word <word> in larger constructions: When <word> is a multi-word phrase
    appearing within a hyphenated construction, it counts only if <word> appears as a
    complete, unmodified element:
         <word> is “New York” → “New York-based” counts (complete element)
    Portmanteaus (words blended from two words where parts of each are combined) are
    NOT included unless the portmanteau clearly contains <word> as a distinct, unaltered
    element:
         <word> is “MAGA” → “MAGA-nificent” counts (MAGA appears as distinct element)
         <word> is “magnificent” → “MAGA-nificent” does NOT count (portmanteau blend)

Dictionary determination: Both the Merriam-Webster Dictionary (available online) AND the
Oxford English Dictionary shall be used to determine compound type and/or conventional
spelling patterns. Inclusion in either dictionary as an open or hyphenated compound shall
satisfy the Payout Criterion. If neither provides clarity (or the word is not included),
determination is based on whether written as single unit (closed/doesn’t count) vs. spaces or
hyphens (open or hyphenated/counts) and/or context and previous usage.

PHONETIC EQUIVALENCIES

    Homophones (same sound, different spelling) do not count:
        <word> is “write” → “right” does not count
    Homonyms and homographs (same spelling) COUNT regardless of meaning or
    pronunciation:
        <word> is “bass” → both “bass guitar” and “bass fishing” count
    Proper and common nouns are treated identically:
        <word> is “Apple” → both “Apple Inc.” and “apple” (fruit) count
        <word> is “Will” → both “Will Smith” and “I will go” count
    Minor unintentional mispronunciations COUNT if context makes intent clear:
        <word> is “Mamdani” → “Mandani” counts
    Severe mispronunciations that make <word> unrecognizable do NOT count.
    Trademarked spellings do not count, even when pronounced identically, unless the word
 itself is the same:
      <word> is “lift” → “Lyft” (pronounced “lift”) does not count
      <word> is “uber” → “Uber” counts
Words with non-standard transliteration to English count within reason, provided they are
phonetically equivalent:
      <word> is “Zelensky” → any of “Zelenski,” “Zelenskii,” “Zelensky,” or “Zelenskiy”
      count if pronounced equivalently
Contractions do not count for their constituent words unless otherwise explicitly specified
by the Exchange:
      <word> is “not” → “won’t,” “can’t,” “don’t,” “isn’t” do not count
Numbers spoken as words COUNT, when spoken exactly (not part of a larger number):
      <word> is “five” → saying “five” or reading “$5” aloud as “five dollars” counts,
      saying “five hundred” does NOT count
      <word> is “410” → “four hundred ten”, “four one zero” or “four ten” counts
Ordinals COUNT when <word> is written numerically OR a phrase that includes a
number:
      <word> is “January 6” → “January 6th” counts
      <word> is “3” (written numerically) → “3rd” (spoken as “third”) counts
      <word> is “Eight War” → “8 Wars” and “Eighth War” will count
Cardinal and ordinal number words are distinct:
      <word> is “one” → “first” does NOT count
      <word> is “first” → “one” does NOT count
Acronyms and abbreviations count when pronunciation or letter-by-letter spelling
matches <word>:
      <word> is “NASA” → “NASA” (pronounced “NAH-suh”) counts
      <word> is “NASA” → “N-A-S-A” (spelled out) counts
Acronyms and abbreviations count as <word> when pronounced as the full word:
      <word> is “Doctor” → “Dr.” (pronounced “Doctor”) counts
Expanded forms of acronyms do NOT count:
      <word> is “AI” → “artificial intelligence” does not count
Words in foreign languages do NOT count unless they are perfect cognates with an
English word:
      <word> is “fire” → “fuego” (Spanish) does NOT count
English words within foreign sentences count:
      <word> is “policy” → “Nuestra policy es clara” counts
<word> in organization/company names counts:
      <word> is “Securities” → “Bank of America Securities” counts
Words in URLs, emails, handles, and hashtags (exclusive of domain structure, email
provider or symbols) are treated as compounds when spoken as complete words, with the
type of compound subject to aforementioned rules:
      <word> is “fire” → “firestation.com” pronounced “fire station dot com” counts
      <word> is “fire” → “wildfire.com” pronounced “wildfire dot com” does NOT count
      <word> is “apple” → “apple.com” pronounced “apple dot com” does count
      When presented in URL, email, handle, or hashtag form, <word> shall be treated as
      a compound, not a multi-word word
Sung, rapped, or poetic words COUNT if phonetically recognizable:
      <word> is “money” → “moneyyy” (extended in rap) counts
      Extreme distortion making <word> unrecognizable does NOT count.
Phonetic alphabet spelling (“Foxtrot India Romeo Echo” for “FIRE”) does NOT count.
 CANCELLATIONS AND DELAYS

   The Exchange may list an “Event does not qualify/occur” strike for any Contract. If listed,
   such a strike refers to the structural conditions of the market, and is NOT adjudicated as a
   <word> needing to be said by or validated by having been said by <entity>. If listed, this
   strike resolves to “Yes” whenever the event fails to occur or qualify under the Payout
   Criterion, or any other circumstance prevents normal resolution.
   If no such strike is listed, and <time period> refers to a specific event, the following rules
   apply if the event is cancelled, postponed, rescheduled, or materially altered (including
   being closed to press) such that no video is released within one calendar day of the
   original start time of the event:
         If the event is cancelled, the Contract will resolve to the last fair price determined in
         the sole discretion of the Exchange.
         If the event is postponed or rescheduled, the outcome depends on whether a new
         date is publicly announced within one calendar day (i.e. if the event was scheduled
         to begin on Monday, any time up to 11:59PM on Tuesday falls within this period) of
         the originally scheduled start time:
               If no new date is announced within one calendar day, the Contract will resolve
               to the last fair price determined in the sole discretion of the Exchange.
               If a new date is announced within one calendar day, the outcome depends on
               when the rescheduled event is set to occur:
                     If the rescheduled date falls within fourteen (14) calendar days of the
                     originally scheduled date, the Contract shall remain open and <word>
                     stated during the rescheduled event will count.
                           The fourteen-day window begins at 12:00 AM ET on the originally
                           scheduled date and ends at 11:59 PM ET fourteen days later, unless
                           an alternate timezone is explicitly specified.
                           Example: <time period> is “during the State of the Union on January
                           15, 2026” rescheduled to January 20, 2026 → words spoken during
                           the rescheduled address count.
                     If the rescheduled date falls more than fourteen (14) calendar days after
                     the originally scheduled date, the Contract will resolve to the last fair price
                     determined in the sole discretion of the Exchange.
         Any Contract that cannot be resolved through the Source Agency hierarchy will
         instead be settled at the Last Fair Price. The Exchange will disclose the methodology
         used to determine the Last Fair Price (e.g., Last Traded Price, Time-Weighted
         Average Price) at the time of Issuance.
         If an event begins but is interrupted, abbreviated, or ends prematurely, words
         stated by <entity> during the portion of the event that did occur will count and the
         market will otherwise resolve.
   If the event occurs within the fourteen-day window but the venue, format, or medium
   changes (e.g., moved to a different location, changed from in-person to virtual (with the
   exception of being closed to press)), words stated during the event will count.
   Special condition: Across both cases, whether an “Event does not qualify/occur” strike is
   listed or not, if the event specified as or in <time period> is a live-stream or broadcast (or
   relayed as such) but the live stream/broadcast does not occur, the Exchange will instead
   use an officially released recording as the Underlying, and may delay resolution by up to
   one calendar day to allow for the release of such a recording.
           If partial snippets are released, Contracts on words that do not appear will be
          resolved to last fair price, and Contracts on those that do will be resolved to “Yes”.
          If no recording is released, the relevant outcomes triggered by the
          presence/absence of an “Event does not qualify/occur” strike shall occur as outlined
          above.

TECHNICAL DETAILS (UNLESS OTHERWISE SPECIFIED)

     When <time period> refers to a specific event, pre-recorded content broadcast,
     streamed, or published as part of that event is included if presented as part of the official
     presentation.
          Example: <time period> is “during the concert” with pre-recorded video interlude
          where entity speaks → words from interlude count
          Example: <time period> is “during the television appearance” with pre-taped
          segment aired during broadcast → words from segment count
     Behind-the-scenes content, rehearsals, sound checks, and unofficial recordings are NOT
     included unless officially broadcast or published as part of <time period>.
     Subtitles and captions that display <word> on screen but are not spoken aloud by
     <entity> do NOT count.
     Sign language interpretation or usage by entity does NOT count as saying <word> unless
     the Contract explicitly specifies that sign language counts.
     Text that entity displays on screen (presentation slides, teleprompter visible to audience,
     signatures) does NOT count unless entity reads it aloud.
     Content generated by AI, deepfakes, or synthetic voice technology impersonating entity
     does NOT count unless the synthetic content is officially commissioned, authorized, or
     published by entity, OR the Contract explicitly specifies that AI-generated content counts.
     If <word> is muted due to technical failure or censorship it shall not count unless
     corroborating contextual evidence (including surrounding speech, or an official
     uncensored recording or transcript) confirms that <entity> specifically uttered <word>
     during the muted period.

Minimum Tick: The Minimum Tick size for the Contract shall be $0.01.

Position Accountability Level: The Position Accountability Level for the Contract shall be
$25,000 per strike, per Member.

Last Trading Date: The Last Trading Date of the Contract will be the Expiration Date. The Last
Trading Time will be the Expiration time.

Settlement Date: The Settlement Date of the Contract shall be no later than the day after the
Expiration Date, unless the Market Outcome is under review pursuant to Rule 7.1.

Expiration Date: When <time period> refers to a specific event: The latest Expiration Date of
the Contract shall be fourteen (14) calendar days after the originally scheduled date of the
event. If the event occurs within the fourteen-day window, expiration will be moved to an
earlier date and time in accordance with Rule 7.2. When <time period> is defined by specific
dates: The latest Expiration Date of the Contract shall be two weeks after the end of <time
period>. If an event described in the Payout Criterion occurs and a consensus has been
reached on all terms, then expiration will be moved to an earlier date and time in accordance
with Rule 7.2.
 Expiration time: The Expiration time of the Contract shall be 10:00 AM ET.

Settlement Value: The Settlement Value for this Contract is $1.00.

Expiration Value: The Expiration Value is the value of the Underlying as documented by the
Source Agency on the Expiration Date at the Expiration time.

Contingencies: Before Settlement, Kalshi may, at its sole discretion, initiate the Market
Outcome Review Process pursuant to Rule 7.1 of the Rulebook. If an Expiration Value cannot
be determined on the Expiration Date, Kalshi has the right to determine payouts pursuant to
Rule 7.1 in the Rulebook.

exhibits.label: PX-3 GovInfo transcript extract
exhibits.text:
Source: GovInfo PDF DCPD-202501162, "Remarks in an Exchange With Reporters Prior to the 2026 Federation Internationale des Associations de Football (FIFA) World Cup Draw".
Date shown in PDF: December 5, 2025.
PDF opening text: "Administration of Donald J. Trump, 2025" and title "Remarks in an Exchange With Reporters Prior to the 2026 Federation Internationale des Associations de Football (FIFA) World Cup Draw". The transcript begins: "[The President's remarks were joined in progress.]"

Material transcript extract from the GovInfo-rendered PDF:

2026 World Cup Draw Event/John F. Kennedy Center for the Performing Arts
Q. Mr. President, what do you think about the crowd?
The President. Oh, look, the new star--[inaudible].
Congratulations.
Q. Thank you.
The President. You're the new star of television and Fox.
Q. Thank you. How are you doing, Mr. President?
The President. I'm doing very well.
Q. What do you think about the crowd here?
The President. I think the crowd is great, and I think the building is great. We've made a lot of improvements over the last 6 months. Taking--the Kennedy Center was in very bad shape. Now it's getting to be really good. And in 6 months, it's going to be incredible with the improvements we've made.
Q. The first time for the U.S. to host since the nineties.
The President. Right.
Q. Are you ready for it?
The President. I'm ready for it, and they're ready for it. And Gianni, who you know--
Mr. Infantino. Yes.
The President. --has done a fantastic job with FIFA. And we're looking to have--you know, we're set--we have already set records for tickets sales. I don't think there's ever been anything like it in any sport. And so, I want to congratulate you.
Mr. Infantino. Thank you.
The President. I don't even have to wait. But major records have been set.

2026 World Cup Draw Event
Q. So can you tell me about this big day today?
The President. It's just a big day, and it's a great sport. And it really is coming to America. And never--nobody ever thought a thing like this could happen. And I'm very happy to say that we've already set the alltime record on ticket sales long before the first ball is kicked. Right?
Mr. Infantino. A hundred percent. One hundred percent.

The President's Interest in Soccer
Q. Should there be a special final: American--[inaudible]?
The President. No, but I got to meet Ronaldo the other day, came to the White House. He was fantastic. He's a handsome guy and a great guy. And my son Barron and him--and they had a big conversation. And my son loves soccer--"football," as you would say. But Ronaldo is great. He's a great guy. He's got the whole package. He's a great player.
Thank you very much. Thank you. [Inaudible]

openings.text:
Council members, this case is not about whether the word "Ronaldo" is hard to count. Under the admitted mention-market terms, it is a straightforward distinct word: if President Trump said it aloud as a standalone unit during the relevant event-based period, the strike should resolve Yes. The real dispute is whether public, event-linked remarks at the World Cup Draw venue immediately before the formal program fall within the market's time period, or whether Kalshi may narrow "during the FIFA World Cup Draw" after the fact to only the staged draw program.

The case packet already fixes the contract we are deciding. The market-page file, ev_2c7c2c8a2d76_market-page, identifies the public market title as "What will Trump say during the FIFA World Cup Draw?," the contract family as PRESMENTION / NEWMENTION, the strike as "Ronaldo," the challenged resolution as No, and the requested resolution as Yes. That file does not itself prove the utterance or the timing; it frames the question.

The governing terms, ev_a373371a8c9c_presmention-terms, supply the legal rule. They define the underlying as words used by the specified entity during the specified time period. They expressly allow the time period to be event-based, including a speech, performance, broadcast, or broader event context. They count a word that appears as a distinct standalone unit, and they include open or hyphenated compound appearances where the word remains distinct. They also contain exclusions: behind-the-scenes content, rehearsals, sound checks, and unofficial recordings do not count unless officially broadcast or published as part of the time period; subtitles or visible text do not count unless read aloud.

Those terms matter because the market title did not say "during Trump's formal on-stage remarks" or "only after the official draw broadcast begins." It said "during the FIFA World Cup Draw." For an event-based market, the admitted rules ask whether the word was used by the entity during that event period as the Exchange specified it, and where ambiguity remains, the Exchange's own terms do not clearly carve out public remarks made at the venue and tied to the event merely because they preceded the formal program.

Plaintiff's proof will therefore focus on three factual points. First, the source record must show Trump actually said "Ronaldo" aloud, not merely that the word appeared in captions, headlines, or onscreen text. Second, the record must place the remarks at the World Cup Draw venue and in the event-linked public setting, not in a private backstage exchange, rehearsal, or sound check. Third, the source chain must be reliable enough under the Source Agency hierarchy to determine the expiration value: a primary transcript, official video, or faithful capture is stronger than a repost or summary.

I have also considered the likely defense theory. The strongest No argument is that "during the FIFA World Cup Draw" means only the formal program itself, and that pre-program reporter remarks are outside the event. That argument would be stronger if the market page or terms contained a clear limiting phrase, or if the only proof were an unofficial clip detached from the event. On the present packet, neither limitation appears. The admitted terms instead contemplate event-based periods and official broadcasts or publications, while excluding only categories that are materially different from public event remarks.

At this opening stage, the existing record does not yet contain the full transcript or video needed to prove the utterance. That gap affects weight, not theory: if the later admitted source material does not show an audible standalone "Ronaldo," or shows only an excluded private/backstage context, plaintiff cannot carry the burden. But if the source material shows public remarks at the World Cup Draw venue immediately before the formal program, and the market documents do not clearly exclude that event-linked segment, the preponderance of the evidence will support resolving the Ronaldo strike Yes.

Council members, the defense begins with the simplest point in the record: the current admitted evidence does not prove the proposition. The market-page exhibit, ev_2c7c2c8a2d76_market-page, identifies the market title, the PRESMENTION / NEWMENTION family, the strike "Ronaldo," the challenged resolution No, and the requested resolution Yes. It does not prove that President Trump said the word, when he said it, whether the relevant audio is complete, or whether the alleged remarks were within the market's covered time period.

The governing terms, ev_a373371a8c9c_presmention-terms, also do not make every same-day venue remark count. They define the underlying as words used by the specified entity during the specified time period. They recognize event-based periods, but they do not say that an event period automatically includes all arrivals, hallway exchanges, red-carpet questions, or reporter scrums before the event begins. The terms instead contemplate that the Exchange may specify what is included for a specific event, and they contain limiting rules: unofficial recordings, behind-the-scenes material, rehearsals, and sound checks are excluded unless officially broadcast or published as part of the time period; captions and visible text do not count unless read aloud; a muted or incomplete segment requires corroborating context.

That is why plaintiff's opening overstates the effect of ambiguity. The question is not whether "Ronaldo" is a distinct word. If spoken as "Ronaldo," it likely is. The question is whether the alleged utterance occurred during the market event. The public title says "during the FIFA World Cup Draw." In ordinary market usage, that phrase points to the draw event or program, not every public comment at the same venue immediately before the formal draw. If Kalshi resolved No, the proponent of Yes must show more than topical proximity. It must show that the relevant source material was part of the covered draw period.

The present packet leaves several material gaps. First, there is no admitted primary transcript or video in the record. Second, there is no admitted source-agency document tying any pre-program exchange to the official draw broadcast or presentation. Third, there is no admitted timing evidence establishing when the formal draw began, when the alleged remark occurred, and whether the market's event period included pre-program remarks. Fourth, there is no admitted custody record for any clip that might be offered later, so the council cannot yet distinguish an official publication from a repost, excerpt, caption, or incomplete audio.

The defense has investigated the likely source path and expects the later evidence phase to turn on official transcripts or recordings, not headlines or snippets. The decisive proof should include the full source context, the title or metadata describing the segment, the audible words, the surrounding timing, and whether the source was officially broadcast or published as part of the draw. A formal program video or run-of-show may also matter because it can show whether the alleged exchange was before the event rather than during it.

If later admitted evidence shows only that Trump said "Ronaldo" in public remarks at the venue before the draw program, that is not enough by itself. Public, event-adjacent, and thematically related are not the same as "during the FIFA World Cup Draw." If later admitted evidence shows that the official draw broadcast or official event presentation included that exchange as part of the covered period, the defense will address that record. But on the current case packet, plaintiff has not carried the burden to turn a No resolution into Yes.

The defense position is therefore narrow and record-based: require proof of the exact utterance, source reliability, and time-period inclusion. Without those links, the challenged No resolution should stand.

rebuttals.text:
Council members, the defense has narrowed the dispute helpfully: it concedes that PX-3 proves Trump used the standalone word "Ronaldo." The remaining question is not pronunciation, source reliability, or whether the word counts. It is whether defense can import a formal broadcast-window limitation that PX-1 and PX-2 do not contain.

DX-1 proves a schedule, not a contract definition. It says the final draw was set for 12 p.m. ET at the Kennedy Center, FOX pre-draw coverage began at 11:30, and the draw would take place between 12 and 2. Plaintiff does not dispute those schedule facts. But DX-1 is a U.S. Soccer informational page; it is not the Kalshi market page, not the listed Source Agency hierarchy, and not a market rule amending "What will Trump say during the FIFA World Cup Draw?" into "what will Trump say between 12 and 2 p.m. ET during the draw mechanics."

That distinction matters because PX-2 uses broader event-period language. It says a time period may be a specific range of time, event, or context, including event-based periods. It then gives limiting rules for things like backstage material, rehearsals, sound checks, unofficial recordings, captions, muted content, and pre-recorded segments. Those specific exclusions show the drafter knew how to exclude ancillary material. Nothing in PX-1 or PX-2 excludes an official, public reporter exchange at the same venue, on the same event, with the FIFA president present, officially titled as remarks prior to the FIFA World Cup Draw.

Defense says the word "during" alone requires the draw to be underway. But that is exactly the ambiguity this case presents. In ordinary event usage, "during the World Cup Draw" can reasonably mean during the public event occasion, not only the 12-to-2 mechanical allocation of teams. If Kalshi intended the latter, it could have used the exact language defense now supplies: 12-2 p.m. ET, formal draw, broadcast only, or excluding pre-draw remarks. The admitted market did none of that.

Nor does PX-3's title defeat plaintiff. "Prior to" establishes chronology relative to the formal draw; it does not establish exclusion from the event-linked market period. PX-3 simultaneously gives plaintiff the key facts defense cannot erase: official transcript, Kennedy Center, World Cup Draw context, and spoken standalone "Ronaldo." PX-4 adds that the White House officially published a page for Trump's attendance at the FIFA World Cup Drawing. That is enough to place the remarks in the official event chain rather than in the excluded categories PX-2 lists.

The defense therefore proves only that the remarks preceded the scheduled 12 p.m. draw window. It does not prove that the Kalshi contract clearly excluded those public event-linked remarks. On a preponderance standard, with the utterance proven and the contract language at least broad enough to include this event context, the Ronaldo strike should resolve Yes.

surrebuttals.text:
Council members, plaintiff's rebuttal concedes the schedule facts that matter: DX-1 places the Final Draw at 12 p.m. ET, pre-draw coverage at 11:30 a.m. ET, and the draw itself between 12 and 2 p.m. ET. PX-3 places the Ronaldo remarks at 11:13 a.m. in an exchange titled "Prior to" the draw. Plaintiff's answer is not factual; it is to broaden "during the FIFA World Cup Draw" into the entire public event occasion.

That is the move the record does not support. The market did not ask what Trump would say at the Kennedy Center, while attending the draw, in connection with the draw, or on draw day. It asked what he would say during the FIFA World Cup Draw. The ordinary boundary of an event named as a draw is the draw event itself, especially where the record distinguishes pre-draw coverage from the draw window and the transcript distinguishes remarks prior to the draw from the draw.

Plaintiff says DX-1 is not a contract definition. True, but it is timing evidence. The contract phrase still needs an event referent, and DX-1 supplies the scheduled referent: the Final Draw at noon, with a 12-to-2 draw window. Plaintiff has no admitted evidence that Kalshi defined the time period as the broader venue occasion or that the 11:13 reporter exchange was included in the official broadcast or presentation.

Nor do PX-2's exclusions create inclusion. Rules excluding rehearsals, sound checks, captions, or unofficial recordings answer what does not count inside or near an event; they do not transform every non-excluded public remark into part of the covered time period. PX-2 says the Exchange may specify what an event includes. Here, the admitted market text does not specify that pre-program reporter exchanges count.

So the dispute is not whether a narrower market could have been written. It is whether plaintiff carried the burden to overturn No with proof that the words were said during the market's time period. The record proves an event-linked utterance before the draw. It does not prove an utterance during the FIFA World Cup Draw. The No resolution should stand.
